extractions.title: CIA officials under Trump discussed assassinating Julian Assange – report | Julian Assange | The Guardian

facts.sourceTitle: CIA officials under Trump discussed assassinating Julian Assange – report
keyFacts: In 2017, senior CIA officials and Mike Pompeo discussed options for abducting or assassinating WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange.
This followed WikiLeaks' publication of Vault 7, a set of CIA hacking tools, considered the agency's biggest data loss.
Pompeo described WikiLeaks as a 'non-state hostile intelligence service', signaling a more aggressive stance.
Some CIA officials requested 'sketches' or 'options' for killing Assange, indicating no apparent boundaries in their discussions.
Assange was sheltering in the Ecuadorian embassy in London from 2012 until his 2019 arrest and fight against US extradition.
US prosecutors charged Assange under the Espionage Act for assisting Chelsea Manning in obtaining and publishing classified documents.
operationalDetails: Discussions on kidnapping or killing Assange took place in 2017 during the Trump administration.
WikiLeaks had published Vault 7, compromising CIA hacking tools.
CIA leadership was reportedly 'completely detached from reality' due to embarrassment over Vault 7 leaks.
Pompeo labeled WikiLeaks a 'non-state hostile intelligence service' allowing CIA operatives to treat it as an enemy espionage group.
Assange was in Ecuadorian embassy protection, then arrested by UK authorities and imprisoned in London fighting US extradition.
motiveFrames: Retaliation and embarrassment over the Vault 7 leak, the largest data loss in CIA history.
CIA sought to neutralize what it viewed as an enemy espionage service publishing classified information.
relationshipTurns: Pompeo and CIA leadership escalated from viewing WikiLeaks as a leak publisher to considering it an intelligence enemy.
The US government moved from seeking extradition to reportedly discussing assassination options.
deterrents: The potential global outrage from kidnapping or killing a civilian accused only of publishing leaked documents.
Legal processes and judicial norms opposing assassination without trial.
exactQuotes: 'They were seeing blood.'
Pompeo referred to WikiLeaks as a 'non-state hostile intelligence service.'
'There seemed to be no boundaries.'

Barry Pollack: 'As an American citizen, I find it absolutely outrageous that our government would be contemplating kidnapping or assassinating somebody without any judicial process simply because he had published truthful information.'

extractions.title: Why the US government’s pursuit of Julian Assange was becoming both damaging and untenable - The Australia Institute

facts.sourceTitle: Why the US government's pursuit of Julian Assange was becoming both damaging and untenable
keyFacts: Julian Assange left the UK after reaching a plea deal with the US government in 2024.
Momentum towards Assange’s release included bipartisan support in Australian parliament and US Congress.
Australian PM Anthony Albanese has repeatedly advocated for Assange’s release.
US President Joe Biden publicly considered dropping prosecution of Assange in April 2024.
Assange was indicted in 2019 under the Trump administration on 17 counts under the 1917 Espionage Act related to publishing classified documents in 2010.
US Department of Justice sought Assange’s extradition to face trial in the US.
CIA officials under Trump reportedly discussed kidnapping and assassination plans against Assange.
The case raised significant First Amendment and free press concerns, as extradition could set a precedent for prosecuting journalists internationally.
Biden administration faced international embarrassment due to continued pursuit of Assange conflicting with democratic values.
The US prosecution strained US-Australia relations, with Australia viewing it as inconsistent with alliance values.
Despite pressure, Assange agreed to plead guilty to a felony Espionage Act charge, leaving concerns about press freedom precedent.
operationalDetails: WikiLeaks published hundreds of thousands of classified military documents in 2010, triggering US pursuit.
Indictment in 2019 on Espionage Act charges for Assange’s role in publishing leaks.
Assange faced extradition proceedings in UK courts under US requests.
Biden kept a distance from DOJ but expressed consideration for dropping charges against Assange.
Assange left UK in 2024 after negotiating plea deal with US government.
motiveFrames: US desire to punish Assange for embarrassment caused by leaks.
Deterrence to prevent similar disclosures by others.
US framing pursuit as upholding law despite free speech implications.
Biden’s intent to restore rule of law post-Trump and maintain democratic values.
Australia-US alliance interest in aligning military and security cooperation with democratic accountability.
relationshipTurns: Growing bipartisan support in Australia and US to release Assange shifted political calculus.
Biden’s public 'considering' dropping charges signaled internal pressure against DOJ’s stance.
US pursuit caused damage and friction in US-Australia alliance based on shared democratic values.
AUKUS security pact intensified US-Australia military relationship while bringing democratic and legal tensions to forefront.
deterrents: Potential erosion of US credibility on free press if Assange prosecuted abroad.
International embarrassment faced by Biden administration in election year.
Legal challenges regarding legal protections for non-US citizens under US law.
Domestic and international political pressure in Australia and US to resolve Assange’s case.
exactQuotes: Joe Biden said he was “considering” a request from Australia to drop its prosecution of Assange.
The US Department of Justice had argued Assange should be subject to US law and extradited to face trial.
CIA officials under Trump reportedly discussed assassinating Julian Assange.
Biden described the 2024 US election as an existential fight for the soul of US democracy.

extractions.title: Alarming reported CIA plot against Julian Assange exposed | RSF

facts.sourceTitle: Alarming reported CIA plot against Julian Assange exposed
keyFacts: CIA allegedly plotted to kidnap or assassinate Julian Assange in response to Wikileaks' 2016 publication of classified CIA hacking tools known as 'Vault 7'.
Plot reportedly orchestrated under then-CIA director Mike Pompeo as retaliation for largest data leak in CIA history.
Over 30 former US officials contributed information to the report published by Yahoo News on 26 September 2021.
Scenarios included abduction, killing of Assange, and extensive surveillance of Wikileaks associates.
The Justice Department expedited drafting charges against Assange to avoid CIA plans jeopardizing prosecution.
Extradition proceedings against Assange to continue in October 2021 in London High Court.
RSF was the only NGO to monitor entire extradition proceedings despite court restrictions.
operationalDetails: Plot included detailed CIA plans for kidnapping or assassinating Assange and spying on his associates.
Plans driven by vendetta following Vault 7 leak revealing CIA hacking tools.
CIA's proposed kidnapping considered illegal and potentially jeopardizing criminal prosecution.
US Justice Department expedited charges to safeguard criminal case against Assange.
Extradition hearing scheduled for 27-28 October 2021 at London High Court.
motiveFrames: Revenge for Wikileaks' publication of classified CIA hacking tools ('Vault 7') causing largest data loss in CIA history.
Prevent further leaks and punish Assange for exposure of US intelligence activities.
Protect prosecution efforts by deterring extrajudicial actions that might undermine legal case.
relationshipTurns: Former CIA director Mike Pompeo allegedly led vendetta focused on Assange due to Vault 7 leak.
Tension between CIA covert operations and Justice Department's criminal prosecution strategy.
Wikileaks and its affiliates targeted by US intelligence and legal systems resulting in high-profile extradition proceedings.
deterrents: Concerns within National Security Council over legality and risk of CIA kidnapping plans derailing prosecution.
Public exposure and investigative reporting highlighting alleged assassination plot.
RSF advocacy calling for US to drop charges and release Assange to protect press freedom.
exactQuotes: "A first in my 30 years as a journalist: reading about CIA detailed plans to kidnap or assassinate Julian Assange and other WikiLeaks staff/associates and wonder if I was on the kill list." - Kristinn Hrafnsson
"There seemed to be no boundaries." - former senior counterintelligence official

"If true, these allegations of a CIA threat to Assange’s life are alarming, and underscore the very serious risk he remains at in detention... The Biden administration must act immediately to distance itself from these shocking reports... close the case against Assange once and for all, and allow for his release from prison before any further harm is caused." - Rebecca Vincent

extractions.title: US intelligence sources discussed poisoning Julian Assange, court told | Julian Assange | The Guardian

facts.sourceTitle: US intelligence sources discussed poisoning Julian Assange, court told - The Guardian
keyFacts: US intelligence and a private security firm, UC Global, discussed plans to poison or kidnap Julian Assange from the Ecuadorian embassy in London.
UC Global spied extensively on Assange, including covertly monitoring meetings with lawyers.
Microphones were concealed in the embassy; Assange’s fingerprint was obtained from a glass.
There was a plot to obtain a baby’s nappy during visits to the embassy, allegedly to establish paternity, which was foiled.
The UC Global founder, David Morales, reported cooperation with US intelligence and described helping US contacts as 'the dark side.'
Morales attended a security trade fair in Las Vegas and secured contracts with US-related entities, notably Las Vegas Sands owned by Sheldon Adelson, a Trump supporter.
Morales traveled frequently to the US with recorded data from the surveillance operation.
In December 2017, Morales ordered installation of cameras with audio recording and livestream capability for US access.
Morales said 'more extreme measures' were suggested by American contacts, including leaving embassy doors open for kidnapping or poisoning Assange.
Stickers were placed on embassy windows to assist 'American friends' using laser microphones, though countermeasures were employed by Assange.
The operation intensified after Trump took office in 2017.
The spying included attempts to monitor Assange’s visitors and his legal defense team.
US authorities were concerned about Assange possibly getting a diplomatic passport to leave the embassy.
The evidence was read in Julian Assange’s extradition trial at the Old Bailey in London, where he fights US charges related to leaked classified documents.
operationalDetails: UC Global contracted by Ecuador government starting October 2015 for embassy security.
Morales's trips to US with recorded data to share with American contacts.
Installation of advanced covert audio and livestream cameras inside embassy.
Use of concealed microphones to record Assange's meetings with lawyers.
Attempt to collect Assange's fingerprint from glass.
Plot to acquire baby's nappy to establish paternity, which was aborted.
Placing stickers on embassy windows to facilitate laser microphone eavesdropping.
Discussion of leaving embassy door open to facilitate kidnapping or poisoning Assange.
motiveFrames: US intelligence aimed to spy on Assange to monitor leaks and movements.
Desire to prevent Assange’s diplomatic passport and potential escape from embassy.
Effort to collect DNA (via baby’s nappy or fingerprint) for intelligence purposes.
‘Dark side’ cooperation between private security and US authorities for global contracts and intelligence sharing.
Preventing Assange from continuing to act against US security interests through WikiLeaks.
relationshipTurns: David Morales transitioned UC Global from low-level Ecuador security to a covert operation collaborating with US intelligence.
Morales reported directly cooperating with US authorities described as ‘the dark side.’
Former UC Global employees feared retaliation from Morales or associates in the US, necessitating anonymity.
Morales was reportedly obsessed with monitoring Assange's lawyers under US requests.
US intelligence increased pressure and suggested ‘more extreme measures’ against Assange and visitors.
deterrents: Former UC Global employees were granted anonymity fearing harm from Morales or US-connected associates.
One UC Global employee refused orders to implement livestream functionality as illegal.
Mother of baby intervened to prevent collection of baby’s nappy for DNA testing.
exactQuotes: 'We will be playing in the big league.' (David Morales on securing US contracts)
Morales said the company had switched over to what he described as 'the dark side'.
'The Americans' had wanted to establish paternity. (Morales referring to US interests)
'He [Morales] showed at times a real obsession in relation to monitoring the lawyers because our American friends were requesting it.'
'More extreme measures' should be deployed against visitors to Assange.
'There was a suggestion that the door of the embassy would be left open allowing people to enter from the outside and kidnap or poison Assange.'
'The document must have been supplied by a third party, which the witness expects was US intelligence.'

extractions.title: UK Appeal Hearing: CIA Reportedly Plotted To Kill Assange

facts.sourceTitle: Countdown to Day X: CIA allegedly plotted to kill Assange
keyFacts: CIA allegedly planned to kidnap, poison, or kill Julian Assange while he was in Ecuador embassy in London.
Extradition of Assange to US threatens his right to life and protection from torture under European Convention on Human Rights.
Investigation by Yahoo News in 2021 based on over 30 former US officials' conversations revealed CIA's plans to abduct Assange.
CIA director Mike Pompeo 'championed' proposals to abduct Assange after Vault 7 publication in 2017.
Pompeo favored a rendition operation breaking into embassy to bring Assange to US via a third country.
Less extreme option involved US operatives kidnapping Assange and handing him to UK authorities.
Spanish security company UC Global, led by David Morales, spied on Assange with alleged CIA support; Morales' laptop contained a folder marked 'CIA'.
UC Global whistleblower testified that Morales proposed leaving embassy door open intentionally as a cover for kidnapping.
Morales' US contacts discussed poisoning Assange.
Senior CIA and Trump administration officials requested sketches and options for killing Assange; assassination discussions occurred at highest levels with 'no boundaries'.
Under Obama DOJ did not prosecute Assange under Espionage Act but CIA rendition plans caused DOJ panic due to lack of legal basis.
By December 2017 DOJ had sealed indictment against Assange during first year of Trump presidency.
Historical parallel: CIA jeopardized DOJ prosecution against Daniel Ellsberg, Pentagon Papers whistleblower, including illegal break-ins and attacks.
Judge William Byrne in Ellsberg case declared events offended 'sense of justice' and dismissed case with prejudice in 1973.
operationalDetails: CIA proposed abduction from Ecuador embassy in London.
Rendition operation likely involving covert break-in and forced removal.
UC Global conducted surveillance and collected intelligence on Assange with suspected CIA collaboration.
Morales suggested leaving embassy door open to facilitate kidnapping under pretense of an accident.
Poisoning of Assange discussed by Morales' US contacts.
CIA and senior Trump officials considered assassination plans and options at top-level meetings.
No legal indictment initially made to cover for covert abduction under Obama DOJ.
DOJ created sealed indictment by December 2017 to provide legal framework for prosecution.
motiveFrames: Retaliation for WikiLeaks publishing Vault 7 CIA hacking tools.
Prevent Assange from leaking classified US secrets and damaging national security.
Discredit and neutralize Assange as a threat to US intelligence and secrecy.
Political motive to silence a dissident and whistleblower exposing government misconduct.
relationshipTurns: CIA director Mike Pompeo actively promoted plans to kidnap Assange after Vault 7 leaks, marking escalation.
Whistleblowers from UC Global turned against Morales, supporting Assange's defense.
DOJ initially hesitated to prosecute under Obama but shifted stance under Trump due to operational pressures.
Historical similarity in treatment of whistleblowers, creating context for current CIA actions.
deterrents: European Convention on Human Rights protecting Assange's right to life and freedom from torture.
Judicial scrutiny by British High Court and appeal mechanisms could derail extradition.
Public exposure and whistleblower testimonies revealing CIA misconduct.
Legal risks and historical precedents like Ellsberg case affecting US government tactics.
exactQuotes: 'CIA director Mike Pompeo allegedly "championed" proposals to abduct Assange after WikiLeaks published the Vault 7 materials in 2017.'
'A less extreme version of the proposal involved U.S. operatives snatching Assange from the embassy and turning him over to British authorities.'

'The word "CIA" appears several times on a Western Digital-branded external hard drive, on which Morales kept the projects and operations that his company — UC Global, S.L. — was contracted to deliver.'
'Christian Morales proposed leaving the door to the embassy open. It would be treated as an "accidental mistake" so that Assange could be kidnapped.'
'Morales’ "contacts in the United States" also discussed poisoning Assange.'
'There seemed to be no boundaries.'
'The bizarre events have incurably infected the prosecution of this case.' — Judge William Byrne

extractions.title: Ecuador to Expel Assange in 'Hours to Days,' WikiLeaks Says | TIME

facts.sourceTitle: Ecuador Plans to Expel Julian Assange From Its London Embassy in ‘Hours to Days,’ WikiLeaks Says
keyFacts: Ecuador plans to expel Julian Assange from its London embassy within hours to days according to a high-level Ecuadorian source cited by WikiLeaks.
An agreement is in place between Ecuador and the U.K. for Assange's arrest after expulsion.
The expulsion is reportedly a response to WikiLeaks tweeting a link to a website alleging money laundering and corruption involving Ecuadorian President Lenin Moreno.
The website linked emails and phone screen captures implicating President Moreno in corruption, which Moreno denied in a nationwide broadcast on Feb. 27.
Ecuador introduced new rules in 2018 governing Assange's life in the embassy, including clauses allowing expulsion if terms were violated, especially regarding public statements damaging to Ecuador.
operationalDetails: Assange was granted asylum by Ecuador in 2012 and has been living in its London embassy since then.
The Ecuadorian government cited public statements and actions by Assange, including tweets linking to corruption allegations, as grounds for possible expulsion.
Ecuador's foreign minister publicly expressed surprise at Assange's and his lawyers' actions last month.
The embassy protocol restricts public statements that could harm Ecuador's government and allows for expulsion if violated.
WikiLeaks relies on an unnamed high-level Ecuadorian state source for the expulsion information.
motiveFrames: The expulsion is motivated by Ecuador's displeasure with WikiLeaks' allegations linking President Moreno to corruption.
Ecuador's government aims to control damaging public statements via embassy rules and to protect its international image.
Assange's actions, perceived as politically sensitive or damaging, prompted Ecuador to consider ending his asylum to maintain internal and diplomatic order.
relationshipTurns: Originally granted asylum in 2012, the relationship between Ecuador and Assange has deteriorated due to his recent public and legal activities.
The introduction of embassy rules in 2018 marked a formal restriction and warning to Assange about his conduct.
The expulsion plan marks a definitive break and deterioration in Ecuador's protection and relationship with Assange.
deterrents: The embassy protocol serves as a deterrent warning Assange that violations, particularly damaging public statements, could lead to expulsion.
Legal challenges by Assange against the protocol were unsuccessful, reinforcing Ecuador's position.
exactQuotes: A high level source within the Ecuadorian state has told Wikileaks that Julian Assange will be expelled within ‘hours to days’.
Foreign Minister Jose Valencia said last month that the government was “very surprised” by that and other actions by Assange and his lawyers.
Moreno, speaking in a nationwide broadcast Feb. 27, denied any wrongdoing.

extractions.title: Ecuador's former president Rafael Correa slams successor Lenin Moreno for stripping Julian Assange's asylum status - CBS News

facts.sourceTitle: Ecuador: Rafael Correa criticizes Lenin Moreno for stripping Julian Assange's asylum status
keyFacts: Julian Assange was granted asylum by Ecuador in 2012 under President Rafael Correa.
Current Ecuadorian President Lenín Moreno revoked Assange's asylum in 2019, leading to his arrest by British police at the Ecuadorian embassy in London.
Rafael Correa called Moreno "the greatest traitor in Ecuadorian and Latin American history" for allowing the British police entry to arrest Assange.
Correa alleges Moreno's corruption, linking Assange's expulsion to exposing Moreno's alleged corruption in leaked "INA Papers."
Moreno asserts the withdrawal of asylum was a sovereign right and due to Assange’s repeated violations of international conventions and protocols within the embassy.
Political shift in Ecuador from Correa's left-wing policies to Moreno's more centrist, social democratic approach.
Leaked "INA Papers" allegedly show Moreno's profiteering from corrupt business dealings.
Moreno is pursuing a more pro-Western foreign policy and distancing Ecuador from the socialist 'Chavismo' approach of Venezuela.
operationalDetails: Assange lived in Ecuador's London embassy from 2012 until 2019 under asylum granted by Correa.
Moreno authorized British police to enter the embassy and arrest Assange in April 2019.
INA Papers leak contained hacked documents related to Moreno's alleged corruption, linked to the timing of Assange's eviction.
Moreno released a prerecorded video announcing revocation of Assange's asylum citing his aggressive behavior and violations of treaties.
motiveFrames: Correa's motivation appears to defend Assange and attack Moreno as corrupt and a traitor for compromising asylum protections.
Moreno’s motive includes aligning Ecuador with international law and pursuing more pragmatic, pro-Western policies, distancing from Correa’s left-wing legacy.
Moreno aims to improve Ecuador’s trade and foreign relations by distancing from controversial figures and policies associated with Correa and Venezuela’s 'Chavismo.'
relationshipTurns: Lenín Moreno was initially Rafael Correa's vice president and chosen successor, but policies diverged creating political conflict.
Correa and Moreno transitioned from allies to rivals, spotlighted by Moreno's decision to strip Assange's asylum.
WikiLeaks tweeted a link to the INA Papers leak which damaged Moreno's reputation, but denied involvement in the publication.
deterrents: Moreno cited Assange's hostile behavior, threats against Ecuador, and treaty violations as reasons making asylum unsustainable.
International law and sovereign rights of Ecuador stated as basis for revoking asylum.
Public accusations and leaked documents against Moreno represent political threats and corruption deterrents.
exactQuotes: "The greatest traitor in Ecuadorian and Latin American history, Lenín Moreno, allowed the British police to enter our embassy in London to arrest Assange." - Rafael Correa
"Moreno is a corrupt man, but what he has done is a crime that humanity will never forget." - Rafael Correa
"Julian Assange was expelled from the Ecuadorian Embassy for exposing Pres. Lenin Moreno's corruption in the #INAPapers." - Rafael Correa

"Ours is a government respectful of the principles of international law, and of the institution of the right of asylum. Granting or withdrawing asylum is a sovereign right of the Ecuadorian state, according to international law." - Lenín Moreno

"The discourteous and aggressive behavior of Mr. Julian Assange, the hostile and threatening declarations of its allied organization, against Ecuador, and especially, the transgression of international treaties have led the situation to a point where the asylum of Mr. Assange is unsustainable and no longer viable." - Lenín Moreno

"Lenín Moreno was Correa's candidate. Correa expected Moreno to follow very similar policies to him. That hasn't happened. Lenín Moreno has pursued a more social democratic, more centrist political line." - Richard Lapper

extractions.title: Assange to be kicked out of Ecuadorian embassy

facts.sourceTitle: Julian Assange to be kicked out of Ecuadorian embassy - DW.com
keyFacts: Julian Assange is set to be expelled from the Ecuadorian embassy in London within hours to days according to WikiLeaks.
Assange has been staying in the Ecuadorian embassy since 2012 to avoid arrest and extradition to Sweden over rape charges, which have since been dropped.
Assange faces arrest in the UK for violating bail conditions by fleeing to the embassy, and faces charges in the US over leaked classified government documents published by WikiLeaks in 2010.
Ecuador blames WikiLeaks for the INA papers offshore corruption scandal implicating President Lenin Moreno and holds Assange responsible for violating asylum conditions repeatedly.
Ecuador suspects WikiLeaks responsible for spreading private photos of President Moreno on social media.
The INA papers revealed alleged money laundering and offshore bank accounts linked to President Moreno and his family, leading to a congressional probe.
operationalDetails: Assange obtained asylum inside Ecuador's embassy to avoid extradition to Sweden and has stayed there since 2012.
Ecuadorian authorities accuse Assange of repeated asylum violations while inside the embassy.
Assange faces potential arrest by UK authorities upon exiting the embassy due to bail violations and US extradition requests.
motiveFrames: Ecuadorian government wants to expel Assange due to souring relations and allegations of his violating asylum terms and involvement in scandals.
US government seeks Assange's prosecution for publishing classified information detrimental to national security.
Assange fears extradition to the US where he faces charges linked to WikiLeaks publications.
relationshipTurns: Initially Ecuador granted Assange asylum in 2012 but relations have worsened over the years leading to Ecuador's intent to expel him.
Swedish authorities dropped the rape charges but UK authorities continue to seek Assange's arrest for bail violations.
Ecuadorian President Moreno has publicly accused Assange of asylum violations and linked WikiLeaks to destabilizing actions against him.
deterrents: Britain has guaranteed Assange will not be extradited to any country where his life would be in danger.
Assange sued Ecuador for violating his fundamental rights by restricting his access to the outside world while in asylum.
exactQuotes: WikiLeaks tweeted late Thursday that its founder, Julian Assange, will be expelled from the Ecuadorian embassy in London within "hours to days," citing a "high level source."
Ecuadorian President Lenin Moreno said Assange "repeatedly violated" his asylum conditions in the embassy.
